The Genitive Case (Γενική Πτώση)

You already know the Nominative Case (Subjects) and the Accusative Case (Objects).

It is time to learn the third major case: the Genitive Case.

The Genitive case has one primary job in Greek: Possession. It is the Greek equivalent of adding an apostrophe "s" ('s) or saying "of the" in English.

1. How to form the Genitive

When a person or thing becomes an "Owner", both the article and the ending of the noun must change.

A. The Genitive Articles

Let's review the articles for the Genitive case. They actually have a very strong pattern!

Gender Singular Owner Plural Owners
Masculine του (of the man) των (of the men)
Feminine της (of the woman) των (of the women)
Neuter του (of the child) των (of the children)

[!TIP] Notice that του is used for both masculine and neuter singular owners. Notice that των is used for ALL plural owners, regardless of gender! If you see the word των, you know instantly that something is owned by multiple people.

B. The Genitive Noun Endings

How do the actual nouns change when they become owners? Let's look at the singular forms:

  1. Masculine (-ος, -ας, -ης): The noun drops its ending and becomes -ου (for the -ος group) or drops its -ς (for the other groups). - Ο φίλος ➔ του φίλου (of the friend) - Ο πατέρας ➔ του πατέρα (of the father)

  2. Feminine (-η, -α): The noun ALWAYS adds an -ς to the end! - Η μητέρα ➔ της μητέρας (of the mother) - Η κόρη ➔ της κόρης (of the daughter)

  3. Neuter (-ο, -ι, -μα): The noun becomes -ου or adds a syllable. - Το βιβλίο ➔ του βιβλίου (of the book) - Το παιδί ➔ του παιδιού (of the child) - Το μάθημα ➔ του μαθήματος (of the lesson)

(Don't stress too much about the plurals right now, but just as a sneak peek: almost all plural owners end in -ων! Example: των φίλων, των κοριτσιών).

2. Possession in Practice

In English, we usually put the owner first: "The teacher's book". In Greek, you must use the "of the" structure: "The book of the teacher".

The thing being owned comes first (usually in the Nominative or Accusative), followed by the Owner (in the Genitive).

Let's look at some examples:

English: The boy's dog. (Or: The dog of the boy). Greek: Ο σκύλος του αγοριού. (Ο σκύλος is the subject, το αγόρι changed to του αγοριού to show ownership).

English: I see Maria's car. (Or: I see the car of Maria). Greek: Βλέπω το αυτοκίνητο της Μαρίας. (Notice how Maria gained an -ς? Η Μαρία ➔ της Μαρίας).

English: The color of the house. Greek: Το χρώμα του σπιτιού.

3. Relationships vs Possession

The Genitive isn't just for owning physical objects; it's also used to express relationships between people, concepts, or parts of a whole.

  • Family: Η αδερφή του Γιώργου (George's sister)
  • Parts: Η πόρτα του δωματίου (The door of the room)
  • Names: Το όνομα της δασκάλας (The teacher's name)

🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist

  • [ ] I know that the Genitive case is used to show ownership or relationships (the "of the" or "'s" rule).
  • [ ] I know the singular articles for owners (του, της, του) and the universal plural article (των).
  • [ ] I know that Greek always structures possession as "The X of the Y" (Το αυτοκίνητο της Μαρίας).
